title: Symmetry-Selective Topological Magnon Engineering by Phonon Angular Momentum
authors: Markus Weißenhofer, Philipp Rieger, Chandan K. Singh, M. S. Mrudul, Sergiy Mankovsky, Peter M. Oppeneer
abstract: Dynamical control of Berry curvature remains an outstanding challenge in the engineering of topological phases. Here, we demonstrate control of magnon band structures via coherently driven phonons, based on \textit{ab initio} spin-lattice coupling and Floquet theory. We show that this control is symmetry selective: linearly polarized phonons leave the spectrum unchanged, whereas circular and elliptical phonons carrying finite phonon angular momentum (PAM) induce chiral interactions that open and tune gaps at Dirac points, generating and reversing topological magnon phases. The gap magnitude and Chern numbers are directly governed by the PAM, enabling handedness-selective topology control. Applied to monolayer CrI$_3$, and supported by symmetry analysis, our results establish driven lattice dynamics as a general route to engineering topological bosonic excitations and a versatile platform for Floquet control of magnetism.
summary: This paper shows that driving lattice vibrations with specific angular momentum can selectively control the topology of magnon bands in 2D magnets. By using circularly polarized phonons, one can open, tune, and even reverse the topological phases of magnons. This provides a versatile platform for the Floquet engineering of bosonic excitations in van der Waals materials.
main_problem: The challenge of achieving dynamical control over Berry curvature and magnon band structures to engineer and reverse topological phases.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that circularly and elliptically polarized phonons carrying phonon angular momentum (PAM) can selectively induce chiral interactions, opening and tuning topological magnon gaps and reversing Chern numbers.
method: The study uses an ab initio framework (SPRKKR, VASP, Phonopy) combined with Floquet theory and a van Vleck expansion to derive an effective time-independent Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The primary system is monolayer CrI3, a 2D van der Waals ferromagnet, modeled using an atomistic Heisenberg Hamiltonian with spin-lattice coupling and Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interactions.
key_observables: Magnon band gaps, Chern numbers, and anomalous thermal Hall conductivity.
important_parameters: Phonon angular momentum (PAM), phonon polarization phase, and the high-frequency phonon regime where phonon frequency exceeds magnon frequencies.
main_assumptions: The reliability of the effective Hamiltonian relies on the vanishing of higher-order corrections in the van Vleck expansion and the high-frequency approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the CrI3 lattice and phonon mode representations; Figure 2 illustrates the modification of the magnon spectrum and Berry curvature by Eg phonons; Figure 3 relates phonon polarization to the magnon gap and Chern numbers; Figure 4 shows magnon bandstructure engineering.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dynamical topological control, presents an ab initio-based Floquet framework, analyzes the symmetry-selective role of phonon angular momentum, demonstrates the results on CrI3, and discusses experimental signatures like thermal Hall conductivity.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates a method for Floquet engineering of topological excitations using driven lattice dynamics, which is highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium many-body dynamics and the control of Berry curvature.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Top view of the atomic lattice of monolayer CrI3. J1 to J3 denote the nearest- to third-nearest-neighbor exchange interactions between magnetic Cr atoms. (b) and (c) Schematic representations of one representative eigenvec- tor from each pair of analyzed degenerate phonon modes with energies and group representations as labeled.

Figure 2. Engineering of magnon bandstructure of CrI3. Ar- rows indicate the opening of a gap at the Dirac points K and K′ upon driving by clockwise circular Eg phonon mode and the color coding shows the Berry curvature of the magnon bands. The inset shows the magnon energy gap versus Cr displacement.
Figure 3. PAM-selective control of magnon gap and topologies in the absence of SOC. (a) PAM and (b) magnon gap at K, K′

Figure 4. Engineering of magnon bandstructure of CrI3 in the presence of SOC, assumed to manifest in DMI. (a) and (b) show the magnon energies around K driven by clockwise (CW) and counter-clockwise (CCW) Eg phonons of different amplitude. Arrows indicate gap widening (a) and closing (b) and the color coding the Berry curvature. (c) and (d) depict the magnon energy gap and the anomalous thermal Hall con- ductivity versus Cr displacement.

Spintronics.note: The paper provides a direct application of quantum-optical concepts (Floquet engineering via circularly polarized driving) to control magnon band structures and topological phases in a magnetic system.
